summary_text:
Chief Complaint
New patient visit for acid reflux.

History of Present Illness
I've just got really bad acid reflux. Though I mean I... right now, I take 40 milligrams of omeprazole twice a day. It feels like burning. Any difficulty with swallowing? Any abdominal pain? Nausea or vomiting, changes in bowel movements? And how long have you had this problem? I've had acid reflux since I was around 12, but it has gotten worse over the past few years. And how long have you been on the omeprazole? Are there any things that make your symptoms worse that you can pinpoint, like food choices or stress? Probably stress and food choices sometimes. We do know that caffeine and caffeinated beverages will do that, even sodas. It's reassuring that you're not having any difficulty swallowing, though. And any family history of GI issues? Um, my grandmother had esophageal cancer. And my mom had some issues like acid reflux, and she was taking some medication that coats the lining of your stomach. Have you ever tried any medication other than omeprazole? Um, at one point I was on omeprazole and ranitidine -dine. But other than that, those are the only medications I have taken. What's the surgical scar here on your abdomen from? My mom said my intestines were twisted, so I had to have surgery when I was around one month old. From what you've told me, it sounds like you've had a several-year history of Gerd, which is the medical term for acid reflux, and recently your symptoms have been poorly controlled with the omeprazole, 40 milligrams twice daily. So the first thing I'd try is changing your diet because these foods can exacerbate your Gerd, even though you're taking the ppi. Stress is another trigger that we need to address. High stress can cause worsening of your symptoms. Yeah, stress and anxiety are definitely big problems for me. So working on that may help with your Gerd. Additionally, we can consider trying a different ppi since the only one you've tried up to this point is omeprazole. If you're okay with this, we can switch your omeprazole to nexium. I will send a prescription for nexium 40 milligrams twice daily to your pharmacy on file.

Findings
The patient has well-healed abdominal surgical scars noted. A normal physical exam was performed.

Assessment
The patient presents with a likely diagnosis of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD).

Plan
I'll recommend that you have a procedure called an EGD, or upper endoscopy, to look for potential causes of the Gerd. We'd sedate you and then pass a small camera through your mouth, down your throat, through your stomach, and end in the small intestines. I'll have you follow up with me in the office for the EGD so we can discuss the results and talk about how you're doing with the dietary, stress, and medication changes. Be sure to call my office and let me know if anything changes or if you have problems before your EGD and follow-up appointment. I suspect that she likely has Gerd with underlying functional etiology as well. If her EGD is normal and her symptoms persist despite a trial of nexium, we can consider a trial of protonix and obtain a pH-impedance study. This will allow us to rule out abnormal acid reflux exposure and to decipher between a functional etiology and hypersensitivity.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Acid reflux.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Michelle King is a 35 y.o. female who presents to clinic today for a new patient evaluation of acid reflux. She was referred by her primary care physician, Dr. Scott.

The patient reports severe acid reflux that has increased in severity in recent years. She explains that she has struggled with acid reflux since approximately age 12 and she describes a burning sensation in her chest that travels up her esophagus. She denies any dysphagia, abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting, changes in bowel movements, constipation, diarrhea, and rectal bleeding. She also denies any unintentional weight loss, although she admits to having a poor appetite due to her being under immense stress for the past 2 months. She has identified triggers for her symptoms, including stress, spicy foods, soda, and citrus or tomato-based products. She admits that she does still eat her trigger foods sometimes despite the exacerbation of her symptoms.

Ms. King has been taking omeprazole 40 mg twice daily for approximately 1 year. She believes that the use of omeprazole provides some relief of her symptoms as there are some days when she is asymptomatic. The only other medication she has tried is ranitidine which she took concurrently with omeprazole and she believes this helped. The patient denies undergoing an EGD within the last 10 years.

PAST HISTORY

Medical
Anxiety.

Surgical
Surgery as an infant for “twisted intestines.”

Procedures
Possible EGD in youth, but none in the last 10 years.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Works as a paralegal.

FAMILY HISTORY

Maternal grandmother with esophageal cancer.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Omeprazole 40 mg twice daily.

ALLERGIES

Seasonal environmental allergies.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

• Constitutional Symptoms:  Positive for loss of appetite.
• Gastrointestinal: Negative for constipation, diarrhea, abdominal pain, difficulty swallowing, nausea, vomiting, blood in stools, unexplained change in bowel habits. Positive for heartburn.
• Psychiatric:  Positive for stress and anxiety.
• Allergic/Immunologic:  Positive for seasonal allergies.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Gastrointestinal
There is a well-healed abdominal surgical scar.

ASSESSMENT

The patient presents today with a several year history of GERD that primarily manifests as heartburn and has been poorly controlled with omeprazole 40 mg twice daily. I suspect that she likely has GERD with underlying functional etiology as well. Reassuringly, she has no unintentional weight loss and denies any dysphagia. The patient has been able to identify triggers of her symptoms, including stress, spicy foods, carbonated beverages, citrus, and tomato-based foods.

• GERD
• Suspected underlying functional etiology of heartburn.

PLAN

• I recommended dietary and lifestyle modifications like cutting out her known trigger foods and trying to reduce her stress levels to help with her symptoms.
• I prescribed Nexium 40 mg twice daily to replace her current omeprazole 40 mg twice daily. I sent this prescription to her pharmacy.
• I also recommended she undergo an EGD. We discussed the potential risks of an endoscopic procedure. The patient understands the risks and is agreeable with the plan. She scheduled this procedure today.
• If her EGD is normal and her symptoms persist despite a trial of Nexium, we can consider a trial of Protonix and obtain a pH impedance study. This will allow us to rule out abnormal acid reflux exposure and to decipher between a functional etiology and hypersensitivity.
• The patient was counselled to call my office if anything changes or if she has any problems before her EGD or follow-up appointment.
• She will follow up with me in clinic after her EGD to discussed results and provide an update on the Nexium.

INSTRUCTIONS

• Avoid trigger foods and try to reduce stress levels.
• Stop omeprazole 40 mg twice daily and start Nexium 40 mg twice daily instead.
• Schedule EGD.
• Call with any changes or problems.
• Schedule a follow up visit after the EGD.

Are there any things that make your symptoms worse that you can pinpoint, like food choices or stress? Probably stress and food choices sometimes. We do know that caffeine and caffeinated beverages will do that, even sodas. It's reassuring that you're not having any difficulty swallowing, though. And any family history of GI issues? Um, my grandmother had esophageal cancer. And my mom had some issues like acid reflux, and she was taking some medication that coats the lining of your stomach. Have you ever tried any medication other than omeprazole? Um, at one point I was on omeprazole and ranitidine -dine. But other than that, those are the only medications I have taken. So the first thing I'd try is changing your diet because these foods can exacerbate your Gerd, even though you're taking the ppi. Stress is another trigger that we need to address. sections.section_value:
Findings
The patient has well-healed abdominal surgical scars noted. A normal physical exam was performed.
Assessment
The patient presents with a likely diagnosis of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD).

I'll recommend that you have a procedure called an EGD, or upper endoscopy, to look for potential causes of the Gerd. We'd sedate you and then pass a small camera through your mouth, down your throat, through your stomach, and end in the small intestines. I'll have you follow up with me in the office for the EGD so we can discuss the results and talk about how you're doing with the dietary, stress, and medication changes. section_miscategorizations.text: Any difficulty with swallowing? Any abdominal pain? Nausea or vomiting, changes in bowel movements? And how long have you had this problem?
section_miscategorizations.reason: These are clinician questions rather than patient-reported history. In a written note, unanswered clinician queries belong to neither HPI nor Findings; including them in HPI as if they were part of the patient narrative is misleading documentation structure.

section_miscategorizations.text: We do know that caffeine and caffeinated beverages will do that, even sodas.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is counseling/education about triggers and management, which belongs in the Plan rather than in the symptom/history narrative.

section_miscategorizations.text: It's reassuring that you're not having any difficulty swallowing, though.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a clinician synthesis of a negative symptom (no dysphagia), which functions as a clinical finding rather than patient narrative; it should be documented as part of exam/review findings, not embedded in HPI dialogue.

section_miscategorizations.text: From what you've told me, it sounds like you've had a several-year history of Gerd, which is the medical term for acid reflux, and recently your symptoms have been poorly controlled with the omeprazole, 40 milligrams twice daily.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is explicit clinician synthesis of the history into a diagnostic impression and control status, which belongs in the Assessment rather than in the HPI.

section_miscategorizations.text: So the first thing I'd try is changing your diet because these foods can exacerbate your Gerd, even though you're taking the ppi. Stress is another trigger that we need to address. High stress can cause worsening of your symptoms. So working on that may help with your Gerd.
section_miscategorizations.reason: These are management recommendations and counseling (dietary modification and stress management) and should be in the Plan, not in the HPI.

section_miscategorizations.text: Additionally, we can consider trying a different ppi since the only one you've tried up to this point is omeprazole. If you're okay with this, we can switch your omeprazole to nexium. I will send a prescription for nexium 40 milligrams twice daily to your pharmacy on file.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a concrete treatment decision and prescription change, which is part of the Plan rather than the HPI.

section_miscategorizations.text: I suspect that she likely has Gerd with underlying functional etiology as well.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a diagnostic impression and differential consideration, which belongs in the Assessment, not in the Plan.
